lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Who meets us here? my niece Plantagenet

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Led in the hand of her kind aunt of Gloucester?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Now, for my life, she’s wandering to the Tower,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: On pure heart’s love to greet the tender princes.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Daughter, well met.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: God give your graces both

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: A happy and a joyful time of day!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: As much to you, good sister! Whither away?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: No farther than the Tower; and, as I guess,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Upon the like devotion as yourselves,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To gratulate the gentle princes there.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Kind sister, thanks: we’ll enter all together.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Right well, dear madam. By your patience,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I may not suffer you to visit them;

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: The king hath straitly charged the contrary.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The king! why, who’s that?

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I cry you mercy: I mean the lord protector.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The Lord protect him from that kingly title!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Hath he set bounds betwixt their love and me?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I am their mother; who should keep me from them?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I am their fathers mother; I will see them.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Their aunt I am in law, in love their mother:

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Then bring me to their sights; I’ll bear thy blame

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And take thy office from thee, on my peril.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: No, madam, no; I may not leave it so:

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I am bound by oath, and therefore pardon me.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Let me but meet you, ladies, one hour hence,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: And I’ll salute your grace of York as mother,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: And reverend looker on, of two fair queens.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: To Anne. Come, madam, you must straight to Westminster,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: There to be crowned Richard’s royal queen.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O, cut my lace in sunder, that my pent heart

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: May have some scope to beat, or else I swoon

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: With this dead-killing news!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Despiteful tidings! O unpleasing news!

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Be of good cheer: mother, how fares your grace?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O Dorset, speak not to me, get thee hence!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Death and destruction dog thee at the heels;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thy mother’s name is ominous to children.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If thou wilt outstrip death, go cross the seas,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And live with Richmond, from the reach of hell:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Go, hie thee, hie thee from this slaughter-house,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Lest thou increase the number of the dead;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And make me die the thrall of Margaret’s curse,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Nor mother, wife, nor England’s counted queen.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Full of wise care is this your counsel, madam.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Take all the swift advantage of the hours;

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: You shall have letters from me to my son

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: To meet you on the way, and welcome you.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Be not ta’en tardy by unwise delay.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: O ill-dispersing wind of misery!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: O my accursed womb, the bed of death!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: A cockatrice hast thou hatch’d to the world,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Whose unavoided eye is murderous.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Come, madam, come; I in all haste was sent.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And I in all unwillingness will go.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I would to God that the inclusive verge

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Of golden metal that must round my brow

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Were red-hot steel, to sear me to the brain!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Anointed let me be with deadly venom,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And die, ere men can say, God save the queen!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Go, go, poor soul, I envy not thy glory;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To feed my humour, wish thyself no harm.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: No! why? When he that is my husband now

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Came to me, as I follow’d Henry’s corse,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: When scarce the blood was well wash’d from his hands

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Which issued from my other angel husband

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And that dead saint which then I weeping follow’d;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O, when, I say, I look’d on Richard’s face,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: This was my wish: “Be thou,” quoth I, “accursed,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For making me, so young, so old a widow!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And, when thou wed’st, let sorrow haunt thy bed;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And be thy wife—if any be so mad—

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: As miserable by the life of thee

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: As thou hast made me by my dear lord’s death!”

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Lo, ere I can repeat this curse again,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Even in so short a space, my woman’s heart

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Grossly grew captive to his honey words

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And proved the subject of my own soul’s curse,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Which ever since hath kept my eyes from rest;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For never yet one hour in his bed

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Have I enjoy’d the golden dew of sleep,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: But have been waked by his timorous dreams.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Besides, he hates me for my father Warwick;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And will, no doubt, shortly be rid of me.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Poor heart, adieu! I pity thy complaining.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: No more than from my soul I mourn for yours.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Farewell, thou woeful welcomer of glory!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Adieu, poor soul, that takest thy leave of it!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: To Dorset. Go thou to Richmond, and good fortune guide thee!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: To Anne. Go thou to Richard, and good angels guard thee!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: To Queen Elizabeth. Go thou to sanctuary, and good thoughts possess thee!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I to my grave, where peace and rest lie with me!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Eighty odd years of sorrow have I seen,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And each hour’s joy wreck’d with a week of teen.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Stay, yet look back with me unto the Tower.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Pity, you ancient stones, those tender babes

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Whom envy hath immured within your walls!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Rough cradle for such little pretty ones!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Rude ragged nurse, old sullen playfellow

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: For tender princes, use my babies well!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: So foolish sorrow bids your stones farewell.

stage_directions.text: Enter, on one side, Queen Elizabeth, Duchess of York, and Marquess of Dorset; on the other, Anne, Duchess of Gloucester, leading Lady Margaret Plantagenet, Clarence’s young Daughter.
And, in good time, here the lieutenant comes. Master lieutenant, pray you, by your leave, How doth the prince, and my young son of York?

glosses.definition: Clarence's daughter Margaret, later Countess of Salisbury — executed under Henry VIII
greet; offer congratulations to
strictly commanded
Richard's pre-coronation title — Brakenbury corrects himself mid-sentence
Westminster Abbey — site of English coronations
cut my bodice-lace in two — Elizabeth's faintness at the news
join Henry Tudor in exile — Dorset's safe harbor in Brittany
Elizabeth recognizes herself as the fulfilled target of Margaret's 1.3 curse
misery-scattering wind — the Duchess's lament-image
legendary serpent hatched from a cock's egg, whose gaze killed — the Duchess's image of Richard
the encircling rim of gold — the crown
the crown become torture-iron — Anne's despair
Anne recalls 1.2 — her first encounter with Richard, when she cursed any woman who would marry him
aged as a widow — Anne was still young but worn out by widowhood

glosses.phrase: Grossly grew captive to his honey words
glosses.definition: was crudely taken captive by his sweet words — Anne's self-reckoning
fearful dreams — Richard's; Anne is already sleeping with a nightmare-ridden tyrant
Anne accurately predicts her own disposal (4.2–4.3)
sorrowful greeter of glory — Elizabeth's oxymoron for Anne's new title
the Duchess's dramatic age — historically about 68 at this point (b. 1415)
grief, trouble (archaic)
walled up, imprisoned
harsh rough nurse — Elizabeth's apostrophe to the Tower as child-keeper

annotations.title: Anne's retrospect — the self-curse she has lived
annotations.body: Anne's speech — "When he that is my husband now / Came to me, as I follow'd Henry's corse... / When, I say, I look'd on Richard's face, / This was my wish: 'Be thou,' quoth I, 'accursed'" — recalls the audience directly to 1.2. Anne tells Elizabeth the curse she laid on Richard's wife-to-be in the wooing scene has fallen on herself: she became that wife. The speech is the play's most searing piece of retrospective self-knowledge, more frank than anything Richard delivers about himself until 5.3. Anne names the mechanism plainly: "my woman's heart / Grossly grew captive to his honey words" — she was won, she knows she was won, she has suffered for it, and she now says so aloud to another doomed woman. The scene pairs with 1.2 as before-and-after: the scene of the seduction, and the scene in which the seduced woman testifies to what the seduction cost.
sources: Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)

annotations.title: Elizabeth's apostrophe to the Tower as nursery
annotations.body: Elizabeth's closing speech — "Pity, you ancient stones, those tender babes / Whom envy hath immured within your walls! / Rough cradle for such little pretty ones! / Rude ragged nurse, old sullen playfellow / For tender princes, use my babies well!" — converts the Tower of London from fortress to child-care metaphor. The Tower becomes simultaneously cradle, nurse, and playfellow — maternal offices grotesquely misapplied to stone walls. The figure is not decoration; it enacts the specific horror of the princes' situation, which is that the institution charged with keeping them is the institution that will kill them. Shakespeare compresses the moral scandal into four lines of apostrophe. The speech is one of the play's emotional peaks before 4.4.
sources: Ann Blake, "Children and Suffering in Shakespeare's Plays" (1998)

annotations.title: The Duchess of York's accursed womb
annotations.body: The Duchess's speech — "O ill-dispersing wind of misery! / O my accursed womb, the bed of death! / A cockatrice hast thou hatch'd to the world, / Whose unavoided eye is murderous" — escalates 2.2's maternal self-reproach into full denunciation. The cockatrice is a legendary basilisk-creature, hatched from a cock's egg by a serpent, whose gaze killed. The Duchess has moved from "he is my son; yea, and therein my shame" (2.2.29) to naming her own womb the instrument of monster-production. Shakespeare does not ask us to accept the metaphysics of monstrous birth — the play is clear that Richard is a product of historical and political violence, not congenital malice — but the Duchess's language registers a particular kind of grief only a mother can carry. In 4.4 she will curse Richard to his face with the play's most terrible maternal curse. This is the speech in which she begins to claim that right.

trials.prompt: Anne's long speech to Elizabeth (62–83) recalls which earlier scene?
options: The wooing scene in 1.2 — Anne admits that her curse on Richard's wife-to-be has fallen on herself

trials.prompt: Elizabeth's apostrophe "Rough cradle for such little pretty ones! / Rude ragged nurse, old sullen playfellow" does what?
options: Praises the Tower for its protection of royal children
Converts the Tower into maternal offices grotesquely misapplied, compressing the specific horror of the princes' situation into apostrophe
Mourns the loss of her late husband Edward IV
Requests formal permission to visit her sons

trials.prompt: The Duchess of York calls her womb "the bed of death" and says she has "hatch'd a cockatrice." A cockatrice is:
options: A legendary basilisk-creature whose gaze was thought to kill — an emblem of monstrous, killing birth
A heraldic insignia of the House of York
